speaker: interviewer
text: Mel, you were telling me earlier that you were going back to Auschwitz, after the war many times, and that you collected a lot of artifacts, a lot of pictures, a lot of documents. Can you tell us a little bit about that and maybe show it to us later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. But also what motivated me to go back to Auschwitz is rather important to understand. If you recall in 1967, a tyrant once again shook his fist and promised to drive the Jews of Israel into the seas. That was Nasser. And that was frightening to us, to all survivors. I remember, we used to just huddle together, and gosh. And then I, I kind of rolled my whole mind back and I said, wait a minute, it wasn't too long ago when a tyrant shook his fist and promised to annihilate all the Jews of Europe. And he did a very thorough job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- I-- says, is that possible? And the world is just sitting idly by. At the United Nations, I was watching all these diplomats and the delegates talk-- nothing. Nobody come to, to Israel's defense, so to speak. Israel was all alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I got to go back to Auschwitz. I don't think this ever existed, so to speak. Auschwitz-- I got to see it and believe it. Well, I did. That was the first time I went back to Auschwitz, was '67. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was very well received by the authorities. And in those days, it was very communistic. As a matter of fact, it wasn't easy even to get to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so I stayed there for about five days. I was put up by one of the officials on the premises, where you could-- the main camp, Auschwitz I. As you opened the window, you could see the tall chimney there. But I, nevertheless, stayed there. I didn't sleep a wink. Five nights, I don't think I slept one minute. But they began to, too interview me. I gave them a number of declarations, depositions. And they also found quite a bit of evidence on my side, what I was and what I was. And I, myself, couldn't tell them much. Because, after all, it was something that I did not live with. It wasn't so close to me, I try to get away from it. And while they enlightened me-- they began to enlighten me, that I was on an [NON-ENGLISH], and now on this barrack, and I was that barrack. And I'm with that Kommando and this Kommando. And I didn't know one from another. But we got so close that they wanted to know information from me, on what routes we took-- the evacuation route and the route from here to there. And I was able to enlighten them. And they-- they were very, very appreciative of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are the officials of the Auschwitz Museum, Polish officials of the Auschwitz Museum, including the director. At that time, it was Smolen, Kazimierz Smolen, who was the director. He is also a, a, a-- a former-- he is a former survivor of Auschwitz. But he is a Polish, Non-Jew, of course, and a communist at that. Oh yes, he wasn't so easy to, to, to, you know, to deal with, to talk with even a lot of times. He was very much on a communistic line. But, then again, on a one-to-one basis, I was at his apartment [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quite often. And then that, that brought up the thing. I said to him, look, what I see here, it's impossible to, to conceive. And I want you to know that very few, if any, Americans will ever, ever come here. What I'd like to be able to do is take a little bit of Auschwitz back here. And he said, I agree with you. But, but, but-- it's very difficult to get anything, any of those things. So I kept asking him, and asking him, and, finally, he did agree, yes. And then I got the barbed wire. I got ashes. And he gave me even a sewing kit. He gave me an umbrella from the victims. And he gave me documents relating to whatever I received from him. And I explained to him what I planned to do with all this is to bring it back here, so that Americans, who are interested in this barbaric event, that they come and see, and then-- and then be sort of you know, helped, assisted, or enlightened, by seeing original items from Auschwitz. And, then, of course, Birkenau, and then I went to different camps. Like when I went to Blechhammer or Jaworzno, I brought it back, first, to Auschwitz, with my friends and showed it to the director. This is what we want, and I'm going to take this with me. He says, all right. If he had said, no, you don't get it. Also posts, you know, these huge posts, he finally shipped them to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you really lived or living here even today, in Auschwitz partially. And you bring your children here and, and your wife--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't bring them, they're here. And you know, that's another thing. I must, perhaps, point that out, that I have never, never insisted that they know that. But they had no choice. And Bernie, the oldest-- I'll never forget when he was still about two, two years old or so, and he, of course, he spotted my tattoo. And he would always sort of, kind of feel it and, what's that, Daddy, you know. And I'm not going to tell him the whole story of Auschwitz. I just told him it's a number. It's a tattoo number. And he kept saying, tattoo, tattoo number. And then as years went by, he knew more and more. And then at the age of 16, believe it or not, he came with me to Auschwitz. I didn't ask him. I didn't even want him to. But he wanted to, so I took him. Then my younger son, who was only 12 years old, he wanted to come. And my wife looked at me and I looked at her. I says, what is this-- a 12-year-old, I can't take a 12-year-old. He wants to go, let him go. So I once brought both of them. And I took them to Buchenwald as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now they are all grown up and you live with all this, what do you tell them? Why do you need this? What is this for? What is your message to them? What do you want them to really understand about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, they see what transpires here. They see students periodically come here, but not, not on a daily basis, only when I can arrange it, like after hours, or if possible, on a Saturday or Sunday. And they know that I'm going to have people here. And, at that time, this place has to be cleared, clean, accessible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is like your little museum, part of Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: You, you, you might want to call it that. But I don't want to call it that. This is an expression of one survivor, who wanted to express himself in this way. I wanted to be able to, to give something to the American fellow man, who is so interested in knowing about the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your message to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- this would be my, my only way of telling them what was done to us, and what man can do to his fellow man. There's no other way. I am not a preacher. I can't do that. I-- I-- as, as my colleagues do, a lot of them, they, they give their time and effort to go to high schools and colleges and tell them over and over and over again. I make every effort to do that likewise. But, in this case, it, it says it all by itself. I don't have to even tell them. When they come here, the students all-- all I am for is to be accessible to answer any question they want.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your grandchild?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandson? He hasn't been here yet. I have not-- I haven't, I haven't got the heart and soul to even begin to tell him that. And, may I if I-- along those lines, I had a little experience about a year and a half ago or so. I was invited to Oxford University, for a speaking engagement. But I was there for about three, four days. And I met with students.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a dinner, I remember, at the Chabad house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one of the young students, well, in his 20s, he, he had his hand across like that. And he says, Mr. Mermelstein, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what would you like me to tell my grandchildren about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, you know, I don't think that is-- that that is to be your role. It should be the duty and the responsibility of the educational system to bring that about. Why should you have to do that, you know. Why should you carry the burden of mankind on your shoulder and then trans-- transform that into your grandchildren. Let them get to know that from their teachers. But sadly to say, and I know it, while we're-- I'm still here, teachers-- those who want to haven't got the tools to do it with. And those who do it already are not well-prepared to tell it the right way. They get it-- a lot of times I hate to say, that mumbo-jumbo. Everything is not a holocaust. What had happened to the Jews of Europe is the [NON-ENGLISH], is the Holocaust. And because those gas chambers, disguised as shower rooms, were built. And they were built specifically for Jews and no other Jews-- no other people, because other people found themselves in there. And that did happen. We know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But to know that those gas chambers disguised as shower rooms at Auschwitz, Birkenau, Majdanek, Treblinka, Sobibor, Chelmno, Belzec, Belzec-- bells might stay til the bells-- they built gas chambers, disguised as shower rooms. For whom? For Jews and Jews only.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what are so difficult to put across, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- to tell-- to tell the teacher to tell the students. They cannot conceive that. They cannot understand that. They have to understand there only one way. This was built for-- Hitler built it for everyone. But it was not the case.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind showing us a little bit of your museum or, or your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- this is one of the posts that was given to me by Kazimierz Smolen, going back years ago, which is a post, you know, from the original post from the camp. And it held the barbed wires all around here. And these are the insulators. And what I did is I turned it into a candelabra, if you note. And it's symbolic. Because six, six, relating to the six million Jews. And then he also gave me that very original sign that says, 'Beware, high tension wire.' And that, that, too, is very precious, because it is original. And when you explain it to anyone that that's what it is, you know, they just don't believe it even, that it was able to, to survive all that weather and what have you. And then here you got some [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Zyklon-B, which I enclosed in there. It's not dangerous. This is original human hair, by the way. That was also given to me. And by the way, all these-- all these things that I just showed you are right here in this document, you see. It's in Polish. It's dated 1978. And Director Kazimierz Smolen signed it. It is a translation of it. And then as we go along here, you see original barbwire from Auschwitz and the insulators. And these are human ashes that were-- was given to me. And I just-- as you can tell-- I turned it into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a collage, whatever you want to refer to it. But it's symbolic to the 6 million Jews. And everything that I do here-- it's not so much very personal. There are some personal family items, but I primarily focus on-- for instance here, here's also-- this is an original canister, an empty canister, that, that held Zyklon-B in it. I'd say about two or three of these canisters was sufficient to gas about 2,000. This was given to me also in Auschwitz, going back to 1967. But the ashes-- and what I did is I-- I had this done by someone, as you can tell, an artist, in Hollywood, if you will, who makes it look like a crematorium. And this item is rather interesting, as you can tell. This was done by David Rose. He's a sketch artist, an NBC sketch artist. And I'll never forget, years ago, I go back probably 20 years, he made a number of these things. And he once called me, to find out if I'd be interested in it. And so I said, and, well, I'll look at it. And-- he put Hebrew lettering, and those are the gas chambers. That's what he-- what he is portraying here. And so I asked him what he wanted for it. And I remember, in those days, he wanted $300 for it. So I gave him the $300.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another one. This is from Buchenwald, because I was liberated in Buchenwald, of course. And so when I got it over here, I extended it into a full size post, barbwire, electrified barbwire post. Those shoes, children's shoes are also given to me by the director, Kazimierz Smolen, from Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And that I, I believe, if I'm not mistaken, is an original insulator from Canada. Birkenau Canada, that's called. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This I did, Birkenau. And it's all original, from Birkenau. And number five is I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I discovered later that after '67, when I put two and two together, I was able to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ascertain that my mother and my two sisters and a group of women and children when I saw them at dawn ended up in gas chambers in crematoria number five. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is also an original post from Auschwitz-Birkenau. Here are some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bowls, you know, very well kept. Believe it or not, here you can tell that it was barrack number 22. This is, of course, a winter uniform that, that we were given in the wintertime to wear. And here you see how we were rounded up. It's, of course, it's photographs of Hungarian Jews being rounded up. And here the box cars, as they were arriving already into Auschwitz. This is a, a picture that I was able to obtain from [NON-ENGLISH] believe it or not. Yes, and this is also [NON-ENGLISH] These are the transports from the Hungarian transports. And here is a newspaper item that I found, between Hitler and Horthy. Horthy was the-- was the leader of Hungary, fascist Hungary. He was a-- he was quite an anti-Semite. But he, nevertheless, was-- had second thoughts about allowing Hitler to take the Jews to where he knew where they were taken to, which was the death camps. These are newspaper clippings that I was able to obtain, going back to the '60s and 70's. And the reason why I got all these is because that it tells a great deal of that period. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These are also items from Auschwitz. And, as of late, I began to turn them into collages. Whether, whether it's of any value or not is immaterial, but it's original. And I know that it-- it's an expression of my own self, as to what I see in those, in those utensils that the victims had brought along with them. Here is a whole set of money that was used in the ghettos. And that was in the Lodz ghetto and other Polish ghettos. That's a very interesting picture. Here is Mordechai Anielewicz. He was the hero and the, the one who had a lot to do with organizing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sort of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Warsaw--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Warsaw uprising.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the head of the uprising in the Warsaw ghetto. And here are some pictures of of some-- some fighters, right here. These are Jewish. They were able to arm themselves and fight back. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a write-up on the slaughter of 125,000. I think this is about Babi Yar. And the date is Monday, November 29, 1943. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There are some pictures, very famous pictures you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's show--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there's a bigger one right there. And then of course, here [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you see, it's a very sad sort of a view to see [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, when you look at-- when you try to find out what ghettos and how they were run. Of course, you might think that these, these police that have Jewish-- you know, the Star of David arm bands, that they were against Jews. They were not. That was just one of the-- the system has to be understood. This whole barbaric, you know, era is one that is very difficult to understand. This is also from Auschwitz. I came across some buttons that were in the ground. And I dug them up, took them to the museum and asked them to give them to me. And this is what I was able to make out of it. That's a poster that I believe was, was done by the Polish, in, in about, in the '70s, late '70s or early '80s. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I like that collage. That was-- took me quite a while to do that. This one, of course, as you can see, the writing in it says, Arbeit Macht Frei, which of course is the cynical, sort of German expression, that if you work, you know, you will be-- everything will be all right. But that's what I was able to-- well, this is what came out of me, when I put this together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's show that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as you note, the yellow part of it is the-- is the Ten Commandments. Because Arbeit Macht Frei was a commandment to us. And here is the lady, if you note, it's a very famous picture-- pathetic woman. And here, these two are original-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] these are original--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Yellow stars that were worn by the Jews. Jude, that's German. And Jood is in Scandinavian. Speaking of Scandinavians, you have, of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you see, Ann-- Annie, Annie Frank. And she, of course, ended up in Auschwitz and then finally in Bergen-Belsen. If I'm not mistaken, she died there. These, again, were made from utensils. That's what I put together here.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a [INAUDIBLE]. It's interesting document right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is a shirt. It says, [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is a shirt. And it was given to me, again, by, by Kazimierz Smolen. And I begged him for it, in fact. And, finally, if you want to open that up, it's practically nothing. But I put it together. Yeah, this is a sewing kit that was used by the victims in Auschwitz. And, again, it was given to me by the director. That item is one for the books, 'Women for breeding, says, Hitler.' And I think the women's movement would not be so happy with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there, too. As you can tell, these documents speaks for-- speak for themselves. I have the original one. Likewise, that one too, that came from Germany. 'The Beast of Man, What is He?' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe this was something that was published here in this country. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an original painting and three sketches done by a former captain. He apparently was with the liberating Armed Forces of the United States. This is another post, wooden, this insulator. And as you can tell, the name is Blechhammer. That was another camp somewhere. It was also a death camp. They had-- they had crematoriums there. But I was there, in Blechhammer, only one day and one night. I was also in other camps, like Gross-Rosen. And we're going to come to, also one day and one night. But it made a tremendous impact on me. These are from Auschwitz. And this, too, utensils, put together. In my mind, it belonged to the Jews. And that's what I did. I made a Star of David out of it. These are medicine bottles that the victims brought along with them. And again, also utensils and-- and-- and other razors and whatever else. Now, as you can see here, I began to turn them into collages, you know. It-- it tells a great deal, as far as I'm concerned, for myself. And I would hope that whoever sees these things doesn't, doesn't think that I try to sort of become artistic with all that. But it's just an expression, my own expression. Here again, Blechhammer. Yeah, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those, those are the type of shoes. That is a lady's shoe, I think. And the hat is a winter cap that we wore. This likewise is Blechhammer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There are so many facets to that, and I can't go into detail. Like, for instance, these insulators are totally different from the ones that existed in Auschwitz. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then, of course, you've got children's shoes. Some sketches, if you note, they were made by a former prisoner, but after, years after. And he did that for me, especially. But the originals that he made before are at the Auschwitz Museum.
